inlineContent.text: Use the Fluent ORM to connect a PostgreSQL database to the tutorial app.
Open a new terminal and run docker compose to start a Postgres server.
Create tables with Fluent in code as a Model and then use Migrations in the database. You configure the database connection and learn where to run the migrations for the app.
to add the Fluent dependencies to the target.
to a class that will represent a database table.
The class is nested in the parent type
to provide a namespace for the class.
This enables the class to benefit from default implementations of common database operations.
the class to conform to a Fluent
serves as the primary key of the database,
identifies a column of type string called contents.
with stubs to provide the migration to create a table.
to reference the table, while the
methods do the bulk of the work.
The function will configure the connection to the databse and run the migrations.
Choose the URL by either reading it from the configuration or defaulting to something that would work in most development setups.
a URL that provides the connection to the database.
The database migrations will create the table.
the function to run the database migrations
will connect to the database using the URL and run the migrations before the app initializes the server.

inlineContent.text: Build on the OpenAPI stubs built in a previous section to add persistence to your OpenAPI routes.
As part of the OpenAPI generated types, we have the stubs of the methods we need to implement for our routes. In the previous chapter, we had returned a 500 status code for the create todo route.
the idea for creating a new TODO.
method to Instantiate a new TODO object from the database.
the code to read from the postgres database.
Review the commented code to evalute the idea for listing all of the TODOs from the database.
the idea for listing all the available TODOs.
Fetch TODOs from the database and store them in an array using the code on the right.
fetching and returning the TODOs from the database.

inlineContent.text: Using a terminal, run the updated Vapor app and try it out!
To test your work, run the commands on the right in 2 separate terminals.
In this first terminal, run the server.
In a second terminal, run a

inlineContent.code: % curl -i -X POST \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{ "contents": "my first todo" }' \ localhost:8080/api/todos

inlineContent.text: and shows the new TODO created.
the output in the second terminal.
After creating the new TODO, make a
request to retrieve all the TODOs.
You should see the todo you created earlier.

doc://com.apple.ASE.getting-started/tuto.title: Clone and build the tutorial repository

abstract.text: Ensure you have the right tools and example code before learning how to write your Swift server application.

The tutorial example contains a Package.swift file that defines the dependencies and targets for the app, as well as a Sources directory with starter files. The finished tutorial is also provided in the Completed folder.
